role_summary: Civil rights organizer in Claiborne County, Mississippi; participant in the Port Gibson Movement, the Port Gibson Merchant Boycotts (the 1966-1970s boycotts that produced NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware, the 1982 Supreme Court case affirming the boycotts' First Amendment protection), organized protests at Alcorn College, and voter registration activities; U.S. Navy Steward Mate during World War II (drafted 1944).
ai_reading: Jones's top semantic neighbor is Charles McLaurin (entry 17) at a cosine count of 124; the broader cluster includes James Oscar Jones (entry 57), Peggy Jean Connor (entry 96), and Clarence Magee (entry 21). The McLaurin pairing is the catalog's clearest example of a Claiborne-County-and-Sunflower-County Mississippi cross-region cosine: both worked Mississippi voter-registration organizing in the early 1960s, and the embedding catches the field-organizing-in-Mississippi register as a stronger signal than either's pairing with the national NAACP or SNCC headquarters voices. The novel observation his page surfaces is the specific-county-vs-statewide-narrative signature: Jones's Port Gibson and Claiborne County work produced one of the most important civil rights legal cases of the era (NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware, 1982), but the historiography often treats Claiborne County as a single-event location rather than as a sustained-organizing site; the embedding's cosine pattern reads his testimony as part of the continuous-organizing tradition the case rests on.
biographical_paragraph: While Jones's LoC oral history carries a not-auditable audit tier (the catalog cannot independently certify the transcript against primary sources for this entry, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), the external biographical record of the Port Gibson Movement is documented in the Mississippi Encyclopedia and the Mississippi Department of Archives and History collections [src: 1, src: 2]. Jones was born in 1914 in Claiborne County, Mississippi. He was drafted into the U.S. Navy in 1944 during World War II and served as a Steward Mate, the segregated Navy assignment open to Black sailors at that date; he describes the racial discrimination of that role in his oral history. He returned to Claiborne County after the war and joined the Port Gibson Movement, the local NAACP-led civil rights organizing that across 1966-1970s ran the Port Gibson Merchant Boycotts to compel desegregation of the town's businesses [src: 2]. The boycotts and associated protests at Alcorn College (the historically Black state university in nearby Lorman, where Jones helped organize student protests) produced the litigation that the United States Supreme Court eventually decided as NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co. (1982), the case that affirmed the boycotts' First Amendment protection and limited the tort liability of civil rights organizers for economic damages caused by lawful boycotts. He continued voter registration organizing across the same arc.
movement_context: The Port Gibson Merchant Boycotts opened on April 1, 1966 in Claiborne County, a majority-Black rural county in southwestern Mississippi whose NAACP branch had been founded in the 1940s by Ernest Jones and rebuilt during the early 1950s through a word-of-mouth recruitment campaign sustained under constant threat of economic retaliation [src: 3]. The 1966 reorganization was led by Mississippi NAACP field secretary Charles Evers (brother of the assassinated Medgar Evers) and by Claiborne County NAACP president Henry Burrell, with First Baptist Church pastor James Dorsey also central to the early leadership; the local branch presented Port Gibson officials with a list of demands covering desegregation of public accommodations under the Civil Rights Act of 1964, the hiring of Black police officers and sheriff's deputies, the seating of Black residents on community boards and juries, and the extension of courtesy titles such as Mr. and Mrs. to African Americans [src: 3, src: 4]. When officials declined to negotiate, a mass meeting at First Baptist Church on April 1, 1966 voted unanimously to launch a boycott of white-owned merchants in Port Gibson and Claiborne County, and Evers led more than 450 protesters from the church to the county courthouse to announce the action; Jones was among the Claiborne County organizers who sustained the boycott through the years that followed, alongside Carolyn and James Miller of the NAACP youth chapter at catalog entry 12 and Leesco Guster, a voter-registration canvasser and boycott participant at entry 77 [src: 3, src: 4]. Claiborne Hardware Company and seventeen other Port Gibson merchants filed suit against the NAACP in 1969 in the Chancery Court of Hinds County, Mississippi, seeking damages and injunctive relief; the case moved through the Mississippi courts for a decade and in 1979 the Mississippi Supreme Court held 92 individual petitioners liable for the merchants' lost trade across the boycott years, awarding the merchants $1.25 million in damages [src: 4, src: 5]. The United States Supreme Court reversed in NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co., 458 U.S. 886 (1982), unanimously holding through Justice John Paul Stevens's majority opinion that the boycott activity (consumer non-patronage, public speech advocating non-patronage, peaceful picketing, and the maintenance of NAACP records identifying boycott-violating Black shoppers) was protected First Amendment expression and assembly, and that NAACP liability for the secondary violence committed by some individual boycott participants required proof of the NAACP's specific authorization or ratification of that violence [src: 5]. Claiborne Hardware remains the controlling First Amendment precedent on civil-rights economic boycotts in the United States; Jones's oral history sits within the multi-voice archival layer the litigation rests on [src: 5, src: 6].
legacy_and_reception: Jones's organizing continued well past the boycott's legal endpoint. The Port Gibson Movement transitioned in the 1970s and 1980s from boycott enforcement to longer-arc community institutions, and the Claiborne County voter-registration work he pursued through the late 1960s contributed to the durable Black electoral majority that reshaped county-level office holding in Claiborne County across the following decades [src: 3, src: 6]. Cultural Crossroads, the Port Gibson downtown-improvement and Black-business-promotion organization co-founded by Carolyn and James Miller of catalog entry 12, became the principal local institution carrying the boycott-era organizing tradition into community arts programming and the documentation of movement history; Jones's interview is part of the archival record Cultural Crossroads and parallel institutions draw on when narrating the Port Gibson story to subsequent generations [src: 7]. Historian Emilye Crosby's book-length community study of the Claiborne County movement, A Little Taste of Freedom (University of North Carolina Press, 2005), positioned the Port Gibson organizing within the broader scholarship on rural Mississippi civil-rights work and on the role of women, churches, and armed self-defense in sustaining the boycott; the book received the McLemore Prize from the Mississippi Historical Society and remains the standard academic monograph on the Port Gibson Movement, drawing extensively on oral history with the local organizing cohort that includes Jones, the Millers, and Guster [src: 6]. Within the Civil Rights History Project collection at the Library of Congress, Jones's oral history is catalogued alongside the other Port Gibson and Claiborne County interviews from the same arc, providing a multi-voice archival record of how the boycott was built and sustained at the household and neighborhood level over more than a decade [src: 1, src: 2].

gallery.alt: Claiborne County Courthouse and Confederate monument, Port Gibson, Mississippi
gallery.caption: The Claiborne County Courthouse in Port Gibson, Mississippi, on the National Register of Historic Places (NRHP reference 79003410). On April 1, 1966, Charles Evers led more than 450 boycott participants from First Baptist Church to this courthouse to announce the action that became NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co., 458 U.S. 886 (1982).

gallery.alt: Church in Port Gibson, Mississippi, photographed in August 1940 by Marion Post Wolcott for the U.S. Farm Security Administration
gallery.caption: Church in Port Gibson, Mississippi, photographed in August 1940 by Marion Post Wolcott for the U.S. Farm Security Administration. The earlier-generation Port Gibson Black churches became the institutional spine of the 1966 boycott organizing that Jones joined; First Baptist Church hosted the founding boycott meeting on April 1, 1966.
gallery.photographer: Marion Post Wolcott, Farm Security Administration
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from Library of Congress)

gallery.alt: Street scene in Port Gibson, Mississippi, August 1940
gallery.caption: Street scene in downtown Port Gibson, Mississippi, August 1940. The commercial corridor photographed here is the same merchant district that the 1966-1972 Port Gibson Merchant Boycotts later targeted, and that the 1969 Claiborne Hardware complaint sought damages over.
gallery.photographer: Marion Post Wolcott, Farm Security Administration
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from Library of Congress)
